region_of_origin: China (Xinjiang / Northwest China tradition, served in Singapore Chinese restaurants)
food_subgroup: Fermented dairy dessert / cultured milk product

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for handmade yogurt milk skin (nai pi). FOODON_03301521 (yogurt food product) is used as the closest parent category. The milk skin layer is a distinguishing feature not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Yoghurt) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for yogurt with a baked milk skin topping in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226760005 (Yogurt substance) is the closest parent. The handmade milk skin variant is not individually coded in SNOMED CT.

preparation_method: baked or steamed to form milk skin

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a single cup or ceramic bowl of yogurt topped with a thin baked milk skin layer.

nutrient_source.title: Yogurt, plain, whole milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170886); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for whole milk (FDC ID 746782) for milk skin fat estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Handmade yogurt milk skin is a fermented whole-milk product topped with a thin baked milk skin (nai pi). No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific preparation. USDA SR Legacy plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170886) is used as the primary nutrient base. The milk skin layer adds a modest increment of fat and protein from concentrated whole milk; values are adjusted upward by approximately 10–15% for fat and protein relative to plain yogurt to reflect the milk skin contribution, consistent with published compositional data for milk skin (malai/nai pi) in dairy literature. Sugar content reflects typical added sugar in Chinese handmade yogurt preparations (~5–8 g/100 g). This is an adapted estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of yogurt and dairy products — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for handmade yogurt milk skin. The GI of 36 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (GI approximately 36, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The milk skin layer does not materially alter the glycemic response as it is primarily fat and protein. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (36 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 4.6, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 8.5 × 1.5 = 12.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~45 mg/100 g) and beneficial calcium and potassium
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 45 mg sodium per 100 g and meaningful potassium (170 mg) and calcium (140 mg), handmade yogurt milk skin is a low-sodium dairy option that is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided portion size is controlled and no high-sodium toppings are added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 36) and moderate sugar content from fermentation and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of 36 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.6 per standard serving make handmade yogurt milk skin a relatively diabetes-friendly dessert; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the ~7 g/100 g sugar content, particularly if additional sweeteners are used in the restaurant preparation, and should limit portion size accordingly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3 g/100 g) from whole milk and milk skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Handmade yogurt milk skin contains moderate saturated fat (~3 g/100 g) from whole milk and the concentrated milk skin layer; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this in moderation as part of a diet that limits total saturated fat intake to below 10% of total energy, while benefiting from the calcium and probiotic content of the fermented base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Handmade Yogurt Milk Skin (手工酸奶奶皮, shǒugōng suānnǎi nǎi pí) is a traditional Chinese fermented dairy dessert in which whole milk is cultured with lactic acid bacteria to produce a thick, creamy yogurt, topped with a thin, delicate baked or steamed milk skin (nai pi / 奶皮) that forms naturally on the surface of heated whole milk. The dish is associated with Northwest Chinese culinary traditions, particularly Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia, and has become popular in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture is smooth and silky with a slightly tangy fermented flavour from the yogurt base, contrasted by the rich, buttery, slightly chewy milk skin layer on top. It is typically served chilled in a ceramic cup or bowl and may be lightly sweetened with sugar. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 85 kcal per 100 g, with 4.2 g protein, 4.8 g fat (predominantly saturated from dairy), and 8.5 g carbohydrate including approximately 7 g sugar. It is a good source of calcium (~140 mg/100 g), potassium, and vitamin B12, consistent with whole-milk dairy products. The glycemic index is low (GI ~36), making it a relatively suitable dessert option for individuals managing blood glucose. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-friendly. Clinical considerations include moderate saturated fat content for cardiovascular patients and sugar content for diabetic individuals. It is not suitable for those with dairy or lactose intolerance.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for handmade yogurt milk skin exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170886) with an upward adjustment for the milk skin fat and protein contribution. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain whole-milk yogurt. Confidence is medium due to the adapted nature of the nutrient and GI data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170886) with an estimated 10–15% upward adjustment for fat and protein to account for the milk skin (nai pi) layer, based on published dairy compositional data. This is an adapted estimate; an exact laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant preparation is not available. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): yogurt and milk skin contain no dietary fibre by nature; this is naturally zero for a pure dairy product. 3. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain whole-milk yogurt; no direct GI measurement for handmade yogurt milk skin was found in the literature. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (170.0), calcium_mg (140.0), iron_mg (0.1), cholesterol_mg (18.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.04), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.18), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.4), folate_mcg_dfe (7.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.1), vitamin_e_mg (0.1), phosphorus_mg (120.0), magnesium_mg (12.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (3.0), water_g (81.0) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy whole-milk yogurt data and are not sentinel values; no -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all fields have defensible estimates from the approved source. 5. The food is served at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles in Singapore; exact restaurant-specific formulation may vary. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules.
